title: De Pijp
standfirst: Amsterdam's food capital and most multicultural neighbourhood — where the Albert Cuyp Market has run every weekday since 1905 and the best dinner in the city costs €14.
intro_html:
De Pijp was built fast and cheap in the late 19th century to house Amsterdam's growing working class. The streets were laid out in a rigid grid — long, narrow blocks that gave the neighbourhood its name: "the pipe." The apartments were small, the ceilings low, and the families large. The Albert Cuyp Market started in 1905 because the residents couldn't afford shop prices. It has run on the same street every weekday since.

What makes De Pijp unusual is the specific sequence of communities that settled there. Surinamese and Antillean families arrived in the 1960s and 70s. Moroccan and Turkish communities followed. Then, in the 1990s, art students and young professionals discovered the cheap rents. Today, the neighbourhood runs on that layering — you can eat roti and bara on one block, Moroccan pastilla on the next, and end the night at a craft beer bar in a converted Victorian swimming pool.

De Pijp resists being tidied up. The Albert Cuyp is still genuinely working, not a tourist market — Amsterdammers shop here. The gentrification is real, but incomplete. It's the neighbourhood most likely to surprise you.

todos.title: Albert Cuyp Market
todos.description:
Running since 1905, the Albert Cuyp is the largest street market in the Netherlands and the undisputed heart of De Pijp. Over 260 stalls stretch for 1.5 kilometers, selling everything from fresh North Sea herring and piping-hot stroopwafels to exotic spices and affordable vintage clothing.

It is a sensory experience like no other in the city, where the shouts of market traders mingle with the aroma of freshly fried poffertjes. Saturday is the busiest and most atmospheric day, but visiting on a weekday morning allows you to see the market as the locals do—a place of genuine daily commerce and community.

todos.title: Sarphatipark
todos.description:
Sarphatipark is a beautifully maintained Victorian-style oasis that serves as the 'living room' of De Pijp. Named after Samuel Sarphati, a 19th-century physician and city planner, the park features winding paths, tranquil ponds, and a lush variety of flora that feels worlds away from the surrounding urban grid.

On any sunny day, the park is filled with locals picnicking on the grass, playing football, or simply enjoying a coffee by the ornate Victorian bandstand. It is the perfect place to pause and sample your finds from the nearby Albert Cuyp market while soaking in the neighborhood's relaxed, residential energy.

todos.title: Gerard Douplein
todos.description:
Gerard Douplein is the social epicenter of De Pijp, a bustling square surrounded by some of the neighborhood's most iconic bars and cafes. Its large, sun-drenched terraces are a magnet for the city's creative crowd, making it the premier spot for people-watching and 'vrijmibo' (Friday afternoon drinks).

As the sun sets, the square transforms into a lively communal party, with locals spilling out of bars like Pilsvogel and Het Paardje. It captures the youthful, high-energy spirit of De Pijp perfectly, offering a taste of the city's modern social life in a setting of 19th-century brick architecture.

todos.title: Brouwerij Troost
todos.description:
Located in a magnificently restored 1920s municipal swimming pool, Brouwerij Troost is a masterclass in industrial reuse. The vast, tiled hall now houses the brewery's gleaming copper vats and long communal tables, creating one of the most unique drinking environments in Amsterdam.

They brew an extensive range of artisanal beers on-site, from crisp lagers to experimental session IPAs. The kitchen also serves up some of the best burgers and 'bitterballen' in the district, making it a favorite for broad-shouldered dinners that transition effortlessly into late-night craft beer sessions.

foods.title: Roopram Roti
foods.description:
Roopram Roti is a culinary institution on the Albert Cuypstraat, serving what many believe to be the best Surinamese roti in the Netherlands. The interior is simple and no-frills, but the queue that often stretches out the door is a testament to the quality of their curry chicken, dhal, and house-made bara.

It is the quintessential De Pijp eating experience—fast, affordable, and incredibly flavorful. Whether you choose to eat in or join the many locals taking their roti to the nearby Sarphatipark, Roopram offers an authentic taste of the multicultural heritage that defines this district.

foods.title: Oeuf
foods.description:
Oeuf is a trendy, minimalist bistro that has mastered the art of the 'all-day breakfast.' Located near the Sarphatipark, the restaurant focuses almost exclusively on egg dishes—from perfectly poached eggs Benedict to indulgent truffle omelettes—all sourced from local, sustainable farms.

The bright, airy interior and focus on high-quality ingredients make it a favorite among the neighborhood's brunch-loving crowd. It's the kind of place where you can linger over a mimosa and a perfectly plated scramble while enjoying the slow, weekend-vibe that De Pijp does so well.

foods.title: Calle Ocho
foods.description:
Named after the heart of Miami’s Little Havana, Calle Ocho is a narrow, high-energy bar that brings a slice of Latin American flair to De Pijp. They specialize in expertly crafted rum and tequila cocktails, paired with an inventive menu of 'street food' like ceviche and tacos.

The vibe is dark, intimate, and perpetually lively, with a soundtrack that leans toward salsa and Latin hip-hop. It is a late-night favorite for those looking for something a bit more adventurous than the standard Dutch pub experience, offering a sophisticated yet unpretentious atmosphere.

products.title: Heineken Experience
products.description: The original 19th-century Heineken brewery is in De Pijp — a self-guided tour ending with two complimentary beers at the rooftop bar.

products.title: De Pijp Food Tour
products.description: A guided walk covering 6 tastings — herring, stroopwafels, Dutch cheese, and more in the heart of the market.

getting_there.text: Metro 52 to De Pijp station — just 5 minutes from Centraal Station.
A 10-minute cycle from the Rijksmuseum and Museumplein.
